title: Basics of Arabic Verbs and Prayers: Past Tense and Mafrūʿ Pronouns
summary: The session focused on using past tense verbs in Modern Standard Arabic, particularly with attached pronouns (mafrūʿ). Key examples included forms like 'qara'at' (she read) and 'fahimtu' (I understood), emphasizing the importance of proper case endings. The teacher also stressed recognizing when knowledge is uncertain by saying 'la adri.'
key_points: Past tense verbs with attached pronouns require adjusting harakāt: e.g., قرأت (qara'at) for she read, and فهمت (fahimtu) for I understood.
Proper use of مAFrūʿ in verbs requires removing the object when using personal pronouns: e.g., دخلتا (dakhaltā) for they entered vs. دخلته (dakhaltu hū) for I opened it.
The phrase لا أدري (la adri, I don't know) is emphasized as a humble admission of uncertainty in knowledge, backed by examples from early scholars like Imām Mālik.
Differentiating between direct verb forms and commands (Sighatu'l-Amr): e.g., اقرأ (iqrāʾ, read!) versus قرأ (qara'a, he read).
questions: [00:14:00] Q: How to add pronouns to verbs — A: Attach the dhamīr as mafrūʿ and adjust harakat.
[00:26:00] Q: Why say 'la adri'? — A: Out of humility, as per scholars like Imām Mālik who prioritized unsureness in knowledge.
